methods.name: Stage-Progression
methods.rationale: Applies typical Series D → next-stage step-up multiplier (1.5x) to $1.8B total raised proxy.

methods.name: Peer-Median
methods.rationale: Median across 3 same-sector + same-stage peers in Defense & Security. Range: $1.4B - $9.2B.

public.rationale: Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use
Public-co frontier-tech comp in Defense & Dual-use

private.name: Saronic
private.rationale: Same sector (Defense & Security), same stage (Series D), post-money $9.25B

private.name: Chaos Industries
private.rationale: Same sector (Defense & Security), same stage (Series D), post-money $4.5B

private.name: Epirus
private.rationale: Same sector (Defense & Security), same stage (Series D), post-money $1.35B

data.narrative:
Saildrone, a Series D defense and security company founded by Richard Jenkins, is evaluated against a peer universe of 10 comparable companies—2 public (DRSHF, IDEAFORGE.NS) and 8 private, including Saronic, Chaos Industries, Epirus, Anduril Industries, and Scale AI.

Two valuation methods are applicable. Stage-progression analysis uses the $1.8B proxy derived from $300M+ total raised, applying a 1.5x Series D step-up multiplier to imply $2.6B. Peer-median triangulation relies on three same-sector, same-stage comparables: Saronic at $9.2B, Chaos Industries at $4.5B, and Epirus at $1.4B, yielding a median of $4.5B and a range of $1.4B–$9.2B.

Blending both methods produces a triangulated valuation range of $2.2B (low) to $5.2B (high), with a median of $3.6B. The 2.4x spread between stage-progression and peer-median reflects meaningful dispersion within the defense sector peer set—Saronic trades at 6.6x the Epirus valuation despite matching stage. Key uncertainties include whether Saildrone's autonomous ocean-based systems command a premium within the broader defense category, revenue scale relative to peers, and the stability of private defense company valuations in the current geopolitical environment. The low-end estimate assumes stage-progression anchoring; the high end weights peer medians more heavily.
